impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when those products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., in air-intake systems not covered by the patent), defeating Ingevity's patent-based defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Reinforces that competitors can challenge tying arrangements where patent holders leverage patents to force exclusive purchases of unpatented goods, opening markets for competing suppliers of those goods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring license agreements that condition patent access on exclusive purchasing of related but unpatented products, even where the products are closely related to the patented technology.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of fraudulently obtained patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (granted in favor of Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The court held that whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct by failing to disclose prior CYMAX technology during patent prosecution, and whether that conduct rose to the level of Walker Process fraud supporting attempted monopolization, required trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of Walker Process fraud benefit from the court's recognition that the high evidentiary bar for antitrust fraud claims based on inequitable conduct requires full factual development at trial rather than resolution on summary judgment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions face heightened scrutiny of their disclosure obligations during prosecution, and competitors can more readily survive summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and antitrust claims based on alleged concealment of acquired prior art.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract under Alice step one, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software were proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents are directed to abstract ideas at Alice step one and require further proceedings on step two. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The companion case reversed a contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel, requiring reconsideration of enhanced damages and attorneys' fees.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity and antivirus patent assertions benefit from the court's willingness to find such claims abstract at Alice step one and from the limitation on recovery of damages for foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity space face increased risk that claims directed to software-based anomaly detection methods will be found patent-ineligible, and foreign sales damages may be significantly curtailed.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $2.5 million jury verdict for GoTV, holding on cross-appeal that all asserted claims of GoTV's three patents covering adaptive content delivery to wireless devices are ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content specifications to a device's capabilities. This required entry of judgment for Netflix across the entire case.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies gain protection from patent claims broadly covering server-side content adaptation for different devices, a foundational aspect of modern streaming technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content adaptation and delivery methods face heightened eligibility challenges, potentially invalidating significant patent portfolios in the streaming technology space.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing a recombinant nucleic acid molecule encoding an AAV capsid protein and a heterologous non-AAV sequence are directed to a natural phenomenon under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that the claims to engineered host cells containing specific recombinant nucleic acid constructs are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The court found that the claimed cultured host cells with recombinant molecules are human-made inventions that do not exist in nature, distinguishing them from claims to naturally occurring products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotechnology and gene therapy companies receive strong validation that claims to engineered biological constructs—such as host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules—are patent-eligible, protecting substantial R&D investments in gene therapy platforms.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to develop competing gene therapy vectors may face continued patent barriers, as this decision confirms eligibility of claims to engineered cellular constructs used in gene therapy manufacturing.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, non-institution authority is insulated from judicial review, and the instructions do not bind the Director who retains ultimate authority.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to decline IPR institution without procedural constraints, preserving the Director's discretion to manage the IPR system.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a cost-effective patent challenge mechanism face continued uncertainty from discretionary denials, with no recourse to challenge the PTO's policy framework governing those denials.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement functionality violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen functionality covered by Masimo's patents. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings, sustaining the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watches.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with wearable health technology patents are affirmed in their ability to obtain ITC exclusion orders against major technology companies, providing a powerful enforcement mechanism beyond district court litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating health monitoring features into consumer electronics face significant import exclusion risks when those features are covered by competitors' patents, potentially forcing product redesigns or feature removal.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's finding of nonobviousness and whether the damages award for infringement of a patent covering pre-lit artificial tree technology was legally supportable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL motion on obviousness and its motion for a new trial on damages, upholding the jury's verdict that Willis's patent covering integrated mechanical and electrical connections in pre-lit artificial trees was not obvious and that the damages award was proper.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product industries receive confirmation that jury verdicts on nonobviousness will be sustained where supported by substantial evidence, even for seemingly incremental innovations in established product categories.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the artificial tree and similar consumer product markets face continued enforcement of patents on product assembly innovations that integrate mechanical and electrical connections.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging the federal judiciary exceeded statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund unrelated expenses. The court upheld attorneys' fees awards, incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives, and the district court's jurisdiction under the Little Tucker Act. Hundreds of thousands of PACER users will be fully or substantially reimbursed for fees paid during the eight-year class period.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides substantial reimbursement to businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users, and the precedent supports future challenges to government fee overcharges under the Little Tucker Act.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory obligation for the SBA to award grants to eligible entities in the order applications were received, giving rise to Tucker Act jurisdiction for claims by applicants who were skipped.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses have Tucker Act jurisdiction to sue the United States for unpaid RRF grants. The court held that the statute's use of 'shall award grants' and 'in the order in which applications are received' created a mandatory, money-mandating obligation that the SBA violated by processing and paying applications out of order. The denial of the government's motion to dismiss allows these claims to proceed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that were improperly bypassed for government grant programs have a viable path to recover damages, establishing that mandatory statutory grant language creates enforceable money-mandating obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability to hundreds of businesses for failure to follow statutory ordering requirements in grant programs, which could influence how agencies administer future relief programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent holder's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft involving Azure SmartNIC technology, and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The court found the district court's Daubert analysis was flawed and that Exafer should have the opportunity to present its damages case.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's correction of overly aggressive Daubert gatekeeping that effectively eliminated infringement claims by precluding all damages evidence, preserving access to jury trials on damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face the prospect of retrial where damages experts were improperly excluded, increasing litigation costs and exposure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced patent infringement of surgical device patents and in granting JMOL to the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and grant of JMOL to DePuy in a patent infringement case involving surgical spinal derotation devices. The court found the district court abused its discretion in its Daubert rulings and erred in granting JMOL, remanding for a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Individual inventors and smaller patent holders benefit from the court's rejection of overly restrictive expert exclusions that can effectively prevent patent cases from reaching the jury.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed litigation exposure where expert testimony exclusions are reversed, and the decision signals closer appellate scrutiny of Daubert rulings in patent cases.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) while finding no violation by active respondents, and whether Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's untimely appeal of the ITC's finding of no trademark violation by active respondents but affirmed the limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The court held that under § 1337(g)(1), the ITC properly presumed the complaint's allegations true against defaulting parties and issued a limited exclusion order, without requiring Crocs to separately prove infringement contentions against those respondents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Trademark holders can obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting respondents at the ITC even when they fail to prove infringement against active respondents, providing at least partial relief against infringing imports.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners must strictly comply with appeal deadlines at the ITC, and failure to prove likelihood of confusion against active respondents cannot be remedied by the default provisions applicable to non-participating parties.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of Google's motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, holding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims covering time-shifting and asynchronous playback of multimedia conference sessions were found directed to abstract ideas.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies offering videoconferencing and collaboration tools gain protection from broad patent claims covering fundamental time-shifting and asynchronous playback functionality in web conferencing.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with web conferencing and multimedia collaboration patents face increased risk of early-stage dismissal on eligibility grounds, reducing the value of such patent portfolios.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 but paid at Title 38 base rates are entitled to enhanced overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday pay under Title 38 or Title 5 regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of a class action by IHS nurses alleging they were denied enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims for pay enhancements under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and applicable Title 5 regulations, allowing the case to proceed for further proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal agencies employing healthcare workers under hybrid Title 5/Title 38 compensation frameworks face potential class-wide liability for back pay claims if they failed to apply enhanced compensation provisions to eligible employees, particularly during periods of intensive pandemic-related work.
